articles.title: 🗳️ Political Data Science: A/B Testing & Causal Inference
articles.contents:
GV300 Portfolio

Text Analysis

☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      GV300 Portfolio
    
    
      
  Home


  Text Analysis

      
  MyProject


  
    
     
    GitRepo
  

      
  






🗳️ Political Data Science: A/B Testing
& Causal Inference





📝 Executive Summary
This project demonstrates the application of Causal
Inference and A/B Testing within a political
communications context. By simulating 1,500 website visitors, I evaluate
how different landing page versions—a standard “Policy” page versus an
“Issue-Focused” page—impact supporter signup rates.

🛠️ 1. Experimental Design & Simulation
In this randomized controlled trial (RCT), we assign users to a
Control group (Standard Homepage) or a
Treatment group (Issue-Focused Homepage).

⚙️ Simulation Logic
Control Baseline: 12% conversion rate.
Treatment Lift: +5% (Total 17% expected).
# Set environment
set.seed(2024)
n <- 1500

# 1. Random Treatment Assignment
treatment <- rbinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = 0.5)

# 2. Potential Outcomes (Counterfactuals)
Y0 <- rbinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = 0.12) # Outcome if Control
Y1 <- rbinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = 0.17) # Outcome if Treatment

# 3. Observed Outcome (support_signup)
support_signup <- ifelse(treatment == 1, Y1, Y0)

# 4. Construct Dataframe
party_ab <- data.frame(
  visitor_id = 1:n,
  treatment = treatment,
  support_signup = support_signup
)
# Load the knitr library for table formatting
library(knitr)
## Warning: package 'knitr' was built under R version 4.4.3
# Display the first 10 rows using kable
kable(head(party_ab,10), 
      caption = "Table 1: Preview of Simulated A/B Test Data",
      col.names = c("Visitor ID", "Treatment (1=Issue, 0=Control)", "Signup (1=Yes, 0=No)"),
      align = "ccc")
Table 1: Preview of Simulated A/B Test Data
Visitor ID
Treatment (1=Issue, 0=Control)
Signup (1=Yes, 0=No)
1
1
1
2
0
0
3
1
0
4
1
0
5
0
1
6
1
1
7
0
0
8
0
0
9
1
0
10
0
0



📊 2. Estimating the Average Treatment Effect (ATE)
The ATE represents the average difference in signup probability
caused by the treatment.
📈 Visualizing Group Conversions
library(ggplot2)
library(scales) # For better axis formatting

# 1. Prepare Data
mean_control <- mean(party_ab$support_signup[party_ab$treatment == 0])
mean_treated <- mean(party_ab$support_signup[party_ab$treatment == 1])
ate_hat <- mean_treated - mean_control

plot_data <- data.frame(
  Group = c("Control\n(Standard)", "Treatment\n(Issue-Focused)"),
  Rate = c(mean_control, mean_treated)
)

# 2. Generate Professional Plot
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Group, y = Rate, fill = Group)) +
  # Use a slightly thinner column for a modern look
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.85) + 
  # Add percentage labels directly above bars
  geom_text(aes(label = label_percent(accuracy = 0.1)(Rate)), 
            vjust = -0.8, 
            size = 5, 
            fontface = "bold", 
            color = "#2c3e50") +
  # Custom Professional Colors
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Control\n(Standard)" = "#7f8c8d", 
                               "Treatment\n(Issue-Focused)" = "#2980b9")) +
  # Scale Y to give some "headroom" for labels
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(), limits = c(0, 0.22)) +
  # Professional Titles and Labels
  labs(
    title = "Impact of Messaging on Supporter Signups",
    subtitle = paste0("The Issue-Focused homepage increased signups by ", 
                      round(ate_hat * 100, 1), 
                      " percentage points (p < 0.05)"),
    caption = "Source: Simulated A/B Test Data (n=1500)\nNote: ATE estimated via Difference-in-Means",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Conversion Rate (%)"
  ) +
  # Clean Theme Overrides
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 13, color = "#34495e", margin = margin(b = 15)),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, face = "italic", color = "gray50"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), # Remove vertical grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "#2c3e50", face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

Key Finding: The Issue-Focused homepage resulted in
a lift of r round(ate_hat * 100, 2) percentage points.


🔬 3. Comparative Modeling
To ensure the results are statistically sound, I compare a Linear
Probability Model (LPM) against Logistic Regression.
🔹 Linear Probability Model (OLS)
lm_model <- lm(support_signup ~ treatment, data = party_ab)
Table 2: Regression Coefficients
Predictor
Estimate
Std. Error
t-statistic
p-value
(Intercept)
0.1185
0.0126
9.3891
0.0000
treatment
0.0417
0.0179
2.3348
0.0197
🔸 Logistic Regression (Logit)
The Logit model handles binary outcomes by predicting log-odds, which
I then convert back into probabilities.
logit_model <- glm(support_signup ~ treatment, data = party_ab, family = binomial)

p_control <- predict(logit_model, newdata = data.frame(treatment = 0), type = "response")
p_treated <- predict(logit_model, newdata = data.frame(treatment = 1), type = "response")
Logit Estimated ATE:” 0.0417


👥 4. Demographic Heterogeneity
I analyze whether the treatment effect changes based on visitor Age
or Political Interest using interaction terms.
# Simulate additional variables
party_ab$age <- round(pmin(pmax(rnorm(n, 40, 12), 18), 75))
party_ab$high_interest <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.4)

Interaction Model
Formula : Y ~ Treatment * Covariate
lm_interaction <- lm(support_signup ~ treatment * high_interest + treatment * age, data = party_ab)
Table 3: Regression Coefficients with Interaction
Terms
Predictor
Estimate (Effect Size)
Std. Error
t-statistic
p-value
(Intercept)
0.0853
0.0465
1.8325
0.0671
treatment
-0.0245
0.0646
-0.3793
0.7046
high_interest
0.0047
0.0262
0.1808
0.8566
age
0.0008
0.0011
0.7080
0.4790
treatment:high_interest
-0.0310
0.0366
-0.8470
0.3971
treatment:age
0.0020
0.0015
1.2812
0.2003
Observation: If the treatment:age coefficient is
positive, it suggests that the Issue-Focused page becomes more effective
as the audience gets older.



⚖️ 5. Ethical Implications
In political data science, optimization must be balanced with
ethics:
Transparency: Unlike commercial A/B tests, political
messaging influences democratic choice.
Micro-targeting: Using demographic interactions to
serve specific messages can lead to societal polarization.
Data Privacy: The collection of “Interest Level”
creates sensitive profiles that require high security.


🏁 Conclusion
The data confirms that an Issue-Focused landing page significantly
increases conversion for this fictitious party. Moving forward, the
party should consider a rollout of Version B while maintaining a
commitment to factual accuracy and ethical voter engagement.

            🏛️ Mazna Nasir Almaeeni
          
          
            
              
              Politics & International Relations | University of
              Essex
              
              
              
              
              🌟 Professional Summary
              I am a Politics and International
              Relations specialist at the University of
              Essex, deeply invested in the mechanics of
              diplomacy, international cooperation, and global
              governance. My academic journey is driven by a fascination
              with how diplomatic strategy and leadership strengthen
              relations between nations.
              I am dedicated to mastering the analytical and policy
              skills necessary to contribute meaningfully to global
              stability and international institutions.
              
              
              🎯 2026 - 2027 Goals
              
              “To bridge the gap between academic theory and
              diplomatic practice.”
              
              Professional Placement: Secure an
              internship within a governmental or international
              organization.
              Skills Acquisition: Master advanced
              policy analysis and diplomatic communication.
              Global Perspective: Expand my network
              within the UAE Government Leaders Programme.
              
              
              🎨 Beyond the Books
              I believe in a multi-disciplinary approach to
              leadership. When I am not analyzing international affairs,
              I am focusing on:
              ⚡ Leadership: Engaging in the
              UAE Government Leaders Programme and
              diplomacy-focused initiatives.
              ✨ Creative Entrepreneurship:
              Managing my luxury abaya brand, Atelier Lmzn,
              where I focus on strategic growth and design.
              🌍 Personal Growth: Staying ahead of
              international affairs and building the foundations for a
              long-term career in public service.

articles.title: Assignment 6: Text Analysis
articles.contents:
GV300 Portfolio
Home
Text Analysis


MyProject



☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      GV300 Portfolio
    
    
      
  Home


  Text Analysis

      
  MyProject


  
    
     
    GitRepo
  

      
  






Assignment 6: Text Analysis
Mazna





Introduction
For the text analysis section of this portfolio, I decided to analyze
political speeches. Specifically, I am using the US State of the Union
addresses. I want to see if we can measure the overall sentiment
(positive vs. negative) of different presidents over recent decades.
# Loading the packages needed for data manipulation and text mining
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(sotu) # This package contains the speeches and metadata

# Combine the metadata with the actual speech text
recent_speeches <- sotu_meta %>%
  mutate(text = sotu_text) %>%
  filter(year >= 1990)

# Now we need to tokenize the text (break it into individual words)
# We also need to remove "stop words" (like 'the', 'and', 'a') so they don't mess up our counts
cleaned_speeches <- recent_speeches %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text) %>%
  anti_join(stop_words)

# Match our words with the bing dictionary and calculate a net score for each president
sentiment_summary <- cleaned_speeches %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing")) %>%
  count(president, sentiment) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  mutate(net_sentiment = positive - negative)

# Let's peek at the calculated scores
head(sentiment_summary)
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   president          negative positive net_sentiment
##   <chr>                 <int>    <int>         <int>
## 1 Barack Obama           1204     1583           379
## 2 Donald Trump            628      815           187
## 3 George Bush             278      426           148
## 4 George W. Bush         1197     1654           457
## 5 William J. Clinton     1149     1717           568
# Creating a bar chart of the net sentiment scores
library(plotly)
p <- ggplot(sentiment_summary, aes(x = reorder(president, net_sentiment), y = net_sentiment, fill = net_sentiment > 0)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(title = "Sentiment of US Presidents' State of the Union Addresses",
       x = "President",
       y = "Net Sentiment Score") +
  theme_minimal()

# This makes the plot interactive and animated!
ggplotly(p)
